results.possibleIdentity: Tania (born Tatiana Lewin, 1920–1982) was a Polish-born Jewish American abstract painter, sculptor, and muralist active in New York City from the late 1940s until her death. She is best known for her large-scale geometric public art, most notably a 13-story wall painting created in 1970 at the corner of Mercer and Third Streets in Greenwich Village. In 1966, she was a founding member of City Walls, Inc., a non-profit that pioneered the commissioning of abstract murals in New York. Tania was a participant in the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization, notably featured in the 1968–1969 E.A.T. exhibition 'Some More Beginnings' at the Brooklyn Museum. Her work often utilized geometric abstraction, hard-edge painting, and large aluminum sculptures. She studied at the Art Students League of New York and taught at New York University starting in 1963.

results.possibleIdentity: Evelyn Rosana was a painter and sculptor active in the New York City and Mexican art scenes during the 1960s and early 1970s. In June 1964, she participated in a notable art exhibition and benefit sale held at the Militant Labor Forum (located at 116 University Place, New York), where she exhibited alongside prominent avant-garde artists such as Larry Zox, George Herms, and John Manning. Around the same period, she was featured in an exhibition titled "Cuatro artistas norteamericanas" (Four North American Artists) at Galeria 1577 (Avenida Hidalgo 107) in Mexico City, representing her as a U.S.-based artist working internationally. Her association with figures like George Herms—who was involved with the avant-garde movements that overlapped with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.)—and her profile as a North American artist active in both New York and Mexico align with the timeframe and context of E.A.T. collaborations.

results.possibleIdentity: Michael Davis is an American sculptor and public artist born in Los Angeles in 1948. He has been active since the late 1960s and early 1970s, notably forming the 58F Plaza, a cooperative artist's gallery in Orange County, in 1970. His work often integrates light, architecture, and technology, leading to numerous public art commissions across the United States, including transit stations in Los Angeles, Seattle, and Dallas. He received his MFA from California State University, Fullerton, and has been featured in publications like Artforum and Art in America. His practice aligns with the era's interest in artist-engineer collaborations, and he is listed among Southern California artists active before 1980 in the book 'L.A. Rising'.

Michael Davis (1943–2012) was an American visual artist and musician, best known as the bassist for the influential proto-punk band MC5 (Motor City Five), active from 1964 to 1972. Beyond his music career, Davis was a visual artist associated with the Detroit counterculture scene in the late 1960s. He was later a member of the art and music collective Destroy All Monsters alongside Mike Kelley and Jim Shaw, which was known for experimental, multi-media performances and ephemera that bridged the gap between underground music and contemporary art.

results.possibleIdentity: Ron Cooper (born 1943) is an American visual artist best known for his association with the Light and Space and Finish Fetish movements in Southern California during the late 1960s and 1970s. He was a collaborator with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization; archival records from the Robert Rauschenberg Foundation identify his Los Angeles studio as the site for E.A.T.-sponsored events, including a dance workshop with Alex Hay. Cooper was also a participant in the Los Angeles County Museum of Art (LACMA) Art and Technology program (1967–1971), where he developed proposals for immersive environmental light works. In May 1968, he was featured in an Esquire magazine article (photographed by Andy Warhol) as part of the "Dirty Half Dozen"—a group of radical intermedia artists that included Carolee Schneemann, Yayoi Kusama, and Charlotte Moorman. His early artistic practice utilized materials such as polyester resin, lacquer, neon, and glass to investigate color and light transmission. In the 1990s, he gained further prominence outside the fine arts as the founder of Del Maguey Single Village Mezcal.
